info.title: Stadia Maps Geospatial APIs
info.description: The Stadia Maps Geospatial APIs provide you with the data you need to build awesome applications.

post.summary: Get turn by turn routing instructions between two or more locations.
post.description: The route (turn-by-turn) API computes routes between two or more locations. It supports a variety of tunable costing methods, and supports routing through intermediate waypoints and discontinuous multi-leg routes.

200.description: The computed route(s).

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

500.description: An internal parse error occurred; more details will be included

post.summary: Find the nearest roads to the set of input locations.
post.description: The nearest roads API allows you query for detailed information about streets and intersections near the input locations.

200.description: A list of streets and intersections that match the query.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

post.summary: Calculate a time distance matrix for use in an optimizer.
post.description:
The time distance matrix API lets you compare travel times between a set of possible start and end points.
See https://docs.stadiamaps.com/limits/ for documentation of our latest limits.

200.description: A matrix of times and distances between the start and end points.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included. NOTE: failure to find suitable edges near a location will result in a 400.

post.summary: Calculate areas of equal travel time from a location.
post.description: The isochrone API computes reachable areas within a time or distance constraint. The resulting polygon can be rendered on a map and used for assessing urban mobility, planning, or as a search filter of places within a constrained range.

200.description: A GeoJSON object which can be integrated into your geospatial application.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

post.summary: Calculate an optimized route between a known start and end point.
post.description: The optimized route API is a mix of the matrix and normal route API. For an optimized route, the start and end point are fixed, but the intermediate points will be re-ordered to form an optimal route visiting all nodes once.

200.description: The optimized route, which looks more or less like a normal route response. The only significant difference is that the `locations` may be re-ordered and annotated with an `original_index`.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

500.description: An internal parse error occurred; more details will be included

post.summary: Match a recorded route to the road network.
post.description: The map matching API transforms a recorded route into navigation instructions like you would get from the `route` endpoint. The input can be in the form of either an encoded polyline, or (optionally) timestamped coordinates.

200.description: The matched route(s), which looks more or less like a normal route response, optionally with a `linear_references` key.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

500.description: An internal parse error occurred; more details will be included

post.summary: Trace the attributes of roads visited on a route.
post.description: The trace attributes endpoint retrieves detailed information along a route, returning details for each section along the path, as well as any intersections encountered. In addition to tracing a recording route, this is great for providing just-in-time info to navigation applications, enabling them to conserve resources by omitting info like speed limits upfront that may be irrelevant if the user goes off-route.

200.description: The edges along the traced route with detailed info.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

get.summary: Search and geocode quickly based on partial input.
get.description: Autocomplete enables interactive search-as-you-type user experiences, suggesting places as you type, along with information that will help your users find the correct place quickly.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The place name (address, venue name, etc.) to search for.

parameters.name: focus.point.lat
parameters.description: The latitude of the point to focus the search on. This will bias results toward the focus point. Requires `focus.point.lon`.

parameters.name: focus.point.lon
parameters.description: The longitude of the point to focus the search on. This will bias results toward the focus point. Requires `focus.point.lat`.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.min_lat
parameters.description: Defines the min latitude component of a bounding box to limit the search to. Requires all other `boundary.rect` parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.max_lat
parameters.description: Defines the max latitude component of a bounding box to limit the search to. Requires all other `boundary.rect` parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.min_lon
parameters.description: Defines the min longitude component of a bounding box to limit the search to. Requires all other `boundary.rect` parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.max_lon
parameters.description: Defines the max longitude component of a bounding box to limit the search to. Requires all other `boundary.rect` parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.lat
parameters.description: The latitude of the center of a circle to limit the search to. Requires `boundary.circle.lon`.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.lon
parameters.description: The longitude of the center of a circle to limit the search to. Requires `boundary.circle.lat`.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.radius
parameters.description: The radius of the circle (in kilometers) to limit the search to. Defaults to 50km (search) or 1km (reverse) if unspecified.

parameters.name: boundary.country
parameters.description: A list of country codes in ISO 3116-1 alpha-2 or alpha-3 format.

parameters.name: boundary.gid
parameters.description: The GID of an area to limit the search to.

parameters.name: layers
parameters.description: A list of layers to limit the search to.

parameters.name: sources
parameters.description: A list of sources to limit the search to.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: The maximum number of results to return.

parameters.name: lang
parameters.description: A BCP47 language tag which specifies a preference for localization of results. By default, results are in the default locale of the source data, but specifying a language will attempt to localize the results. Note that while a `langtag` (in RFC 5646 terms) can contain script, region, etc., only the `language` portion, an ISO 639 code, will be considered. So `en-US` and `en-GB` will both be treated as English.

200.description: A GeoJSON collection of autocomplete search results.

400.description: Bad request

get.summary: Find locations matching components (structured forward geocoding).
get.description: The structured search endpoint lets you search for addresses, points of interest, and administrative areas. Rather than a single string which the API must infer meaning from, the structured search endpoint allows you to specify the known components upfront, which is useful in many forward geocoding workflows.

parameters.name: address
parameters.description: A street name, optionally with a house number.

parameters.name: neighbourhood
parameters.description: Varies by area, but has a locally specific meaning (NOT always an official administrative unit).

parameters.name: borough
parameters.description: A unit within a city (not widely used, but present in places like NYC and Mexico City).

parameters.name: locality
parameters.description: The city, village, town, etc. that the place/address is part of.

parameters.name: county
parameters.description: Administrative divisions between localities and regions. Not commonly used as input to structured geocoding.

parameters.name: region
parameters.description: Typically the first administrative division within a country. For example, a US state or a Canadian province.

parameters.name: postalcode
parameters.description: A mail sorting code.

parameters.name: country
parameters.description: A country code in ISO 3116-1 alpha-2 or alpha-3 format.

post.summary: Quickly run a batch of geocoding queries against the search, structured search, or reverse endpoints.
post.description: The batch endpoint lets you specify many search, structured search, or reverse requests at once. Once received, all requests will be processed internally on our infrastructure, improving throughput when you need to do a lot of queries.

200.description: A JSON array of the individual query responses, each annotated with a status code. Individual requests may fail but this endpoint will still return all results. Responses will be in the same order as the input.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

get.summary: Retrieve details of a place using its GID.
get.description: Many search result components include an associated GID field (for example, an address may have a `localadmin_gid`). The place endpoint lets you look up these places directly by ID. Note that GIDs are not stable for all sources. See the [online documentation](https://docs.stadiamaps.com/geocoding-search-autocomplete/place-lookup/) for details.

parameters.description: A list of GIDs to search for.

Note that while a `langtag` (in RFC 5646 terms) can contain script, region, etc., only the `language` portion, an ISO 639 code, will be considered. So `en-US` and `en-GB` will both be treated as English.

200.description: A GeoJSON collection of search results.

400.description: Bad request

get.summary: Get time zone information for any point on earth.
get.description: Retrieve the time zone identifier, standard UTC offset, special offset (such as DST), and the localized time in several common formats.

parameters.name: lat
parameters.description: The latitude of the point you are interested in.

parameters.name: lng
parameters.description: The longitude of the point you are interested in.

parameters.name: timestamp
parameters.description:
The UNIX timestamp at which the UTC and DST offsets will be calculated.
This defaults to the present time. This endpoint is not necessarily guaranteed to be accurate
for timestamps that occurred in the past. Time zone geographic boundaries change
over time, so if the point you are querying for was previously in a different
time zone, historical results will not be accurate. If, however, the point has
been in the same geographic time zone for a very long time (ex: `America/New_York`),
the historical data may be accurate for 100+ years in the past (depending on how far
back the IANA TZDB rules have been specified).

200.description: The time zone information and local time.

400.description: Bad request

get.summary: Get time zone information for any point on earth.
get.description: Retrieve the time zone identifier, total UTC offset, whether DST is in effect, and the localized time in several common formats.

parameters.name: lat
parameters.description: The latitude of the point you are interested in.

parameters.name: lng
parameters.description: The longitude of the point you are interested in.

parameters.name: timestamp
parameters.description:

This defaults to the present time. This endpoint is not necessarily guaranteed to be accurate
for timestamps that occurred in the past. Time zone geographic boundaries change
over time, so if the point you are querying for was previously in a different
time zone, historical results will not be accurate. If, however, the point has
been in the same geographic time zone for a very long time (ex: `America/New_York`),
the historical data may be accurate for 100+ years in the past (depending on how far

200.description: The time zone information and local time.

400.description: Bad request

post.summary: Get the elevation profile along a polyline or at a point.
post.description: The Stadia elevation API allows you to get the elevation of any point on earth. You can pass either a simple set of points or a Google encoded polyline string. This pairs well with our routing APIs, so you can generate an elevation profile for your next bike or run.

200.description: A list of elevations along the polyline, in meters.

400.description: Bad request; more details will be included

get.summary: Search and geocode quickly based on partial input.
get.description: Autocomplete enables interactive search-as-you-type user experiences, suggesting places as you type, along with information that will help your users find the correct place quickly.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The text to search for (the start of an address, place name, etc.).

parameters.name: focus.point.lat
parameters.description:
The latitude of a focus point.

If provided (along with longitude), the search results should be more locally relevant.

parameters.name: focus.point.lon
parameters.description:
The longitude of a focus point.

If provided (along with longitude), the search results should be more locally relevant.

parameters.name: layers
parameters.description: A list of layers to limit the search to.

parameters.name: sources
parameters.description: A list of sources to limit the search to.

parameters.name: boundary.gid
parameters.description:
The GID of a region to limit the search to.

Note: these are not stable for all datasets! For example, OSM features may be deleted and re-added with a new ID.

parameters.name: boundary.country
parameters.description:
A list of comma-separated country codes in ISO 3116-1 alpha-2 or alpha-3 format.
The search will be limited to these countries.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.min_lat
parameters.description:
The minimum latitude component of a search bounding box.

NOTE: Requires all other boundary.rect parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.min_lon
parameters.description:
The minimum longitude component of a search bounding box.

NOTE: Requires all other boundary.rect parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.max_lat
parameters.description:
The maximum latitude component of a search bounding box.

NOTE: Requires all other boundary.rect parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.rect.max_lon
parameters.description:
The maximum longitude component of a search bounding box.

NOTE: Requires all other boundary.rect parameters to be specified.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.lat
parameters.description:
The latitude of the center of a circle to limit the search to.

NOTE: Requires boundary.circle.lon.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.lon
parameters.description:
The longitude of the center of a circle to limit the search to.

NOTE: Requires boundary.circle.lat.

parameters.name: boundary.circle.radius
parameters.description:
The radius of the circle (in kilometers) to limit the search to.

NOTE: Requires the other boundary.circle parameters to take effect.
Defaults to 50km if unspecified.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: The maximum number of items to return from a query.

parameters.name: lang
parameters.description:
A BCP47 language tag which specifies a preference for localization of results.
There is no default value, so place names will be returned as-is,
which is usually in the local language.
NOTE: The Accept-Language header is also respected, and many user agents will set it automatically.

200.description: A GeoJSON FeatureCollection with the results

400.description: A response with a description of why the request is malformed

500.description: A response with a description of what went wrong internally

AddendumV2.description: Miscellaneous data that doesn't quite fit anywhere else in the record.

cross_street.description: The cross street address component (rarely used).

number.description:
The house / building number.

Note that, despite the name, this is not strictly numeric.
Values such as 30-1 and 11A may be valid building/house numbers in some areas.

street.description: The street component of the address.

Context.description:
The geographic context for a feature.

Note that while data sources and country codes are listed,
this does not *necessarily* mean that the view is a specific hierarchy endorsed by that source.
We generally attempt to present the same view as OpenStreetMap.
Contact us if your use case requires a specific political view of the world.

iso_3166_a2.description: The ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code in which the feature is located.

iso_3166_a3.description: The ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code in which the feature is located.

whosonfirst.description: The geographic context, with administrative hierarchy modeled using the Who's on First taxonomy.

Coordinate.description: A coordinate point with latitude and longitude.

DocumentGid.description: A globally unique identifier for a feature. Note: these are not stable for all datasets! For example, OSM features may be deleted and re-added with a new ID. Others like Who's on First guarantee stability long term.

coarse_location.description:
The coarse-grained location of the place (e.g. Seoul, South Korea).

In search experiences, this is typically the second line of a result cell.

confidence.description:
The level of confidence (0.0 - 1.0) that the result is what you actually searched for.

This is not necessarily the same as relevance (results are returned sorted by relevance already),
but rather how closely the explicit or inferred components match the result.
This is only present for forward geocoding responses (not autocomplete or place details).

distance.description: The distance from the search focus point, in kilometers.

formatted_address_line.description:
The address formatted as a single line,
following local postal conventions for ordering and separators.

formatted_address_lines.description:
Address components split up into lines,
following local postal conventions for ordering and separators.

gid.description:
The globally unique identifier for this result.

You can use this to uniquely identify a place,
and to get the full details from the place details endpoint.

NOTE: While GIDs are unique, they may not necessarily be stable in all datasets.

layer.description: The data layer containing the place (e.g. "address" or "poi").

oneOf.description: The type of match (search and structured search only).

name.description:
The best name for the place, accounting for request language preferences.

When building an autocomplete search experience, this is the primary display string.

sources.description: A list of sources from which the result is derived.

GeocodeResponseEnvelope_PropertiesV2.description:
The GeoJSON response envelope.

This is parameterized over the feature properties type
to allow for changes between versions.

bbox.description:
The geographic bounding box covering all features in the result set.

This is empty for autocomplete results.

type.description:
The GeoJSON object type as defined in RFC 7946.

NOTE: This is always FeatureCollection, as the response envelope is designed to hold multiple results.

error.description: Errors encountered serving the request (if any).

query.description:
An object containing all valid, parsed query parameters.

For example, this will contain text and possibly other parameters for an autocomplete query.
This will not be present for invalid requests.

feature_code.description: The GeoNames feature code (for GeoNames results only).

HeightPrecision.description: The decimal precision (number of digits after the point) of the output. When 0, integer values are returned. Valid values are 0, 1, and 2.

shape.description: The input shape, if provided as coordinates.

height.description:
The list of heights for each point, in meters. Present only if `range` is `false`.
Null values indicate missing data.

range_height.description:
The list of ranges and heights for each point in the shape, where each entry is an array of length 2.
Present only if `range` is `true`.

In each pair, the first element represents the range or distance along the input locations. It is the
cumulative distance along the previous coordinates in the shape up to the current coordinate.
This value for the first coordinate in the shape will always be 0.
The second element in the pair represents the height or elevation at the associated coordinate. The height
is null if no height data exists for a given location.
Both values are expressed in meters.

LayerId.description: Canonical geocoding layer IDs for querying.

OpenStreetMapAddendum.description:
OpenStreetMap-specific additional fields.

These values are exactly as they appear in the associated OSM tag.

brand.description:
The principal brand of goods/services sold at a place,
or the common identity for individually owned and operated stores.

opening_hours.description:
The opening hours of the place, in the OSM Opening Hours specification.

See https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Key:opening_hours/specification for details.

operator.description:
The company, corp, person, or other entity directly in charge of operating something.

This is often used for public transport, hotels, restaurants, and postal services.
See https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Key:operator for details

Point.description: A point geometry in GeoJSON format.

Precision.description: The level of detail for the geometry.

coarse_location.description:
The coarse-grained location of the place (e.g. Seoul, South Korea).

In search experiences, this is typically the second line of a result cell.

confidence.description:
The level of confidence (0.0 - 1.0) that the result is what you actually searched for.

This is not necessarily the same as relevance (results are returned sorted by relevance already),
but rather how closely the explicit or inferred components match the result.
This is only present for forward geocoding responses (not autocomplete or place details).

distance.description: The distance from the search focus point, in kilometers.

formatted_address_line.description:
The address formatted as a single line,
following local postal conventions for ordering and separators.

formatted_address_lines.description:
Address components split up into lines,
following local postal conventions for ordering and separators.

gid.description:
The globally unique identifier for this result.

You can use this to uniquely identify a place,
and to get the full details from the place details endpoint.

NOTE: While GIDs are unique, they may not necessarily be stable in all datasets.

layer.description: The data layer containing the place (e.g. "address" or "poi").

oneOf.description: The type of match (search and structured search only).

name.description:
The best name for the place, accounting for request language preferences.

When building an autocomplete search experience, this is the primary display string.

sources.description: A list of sources from which the result is derived.

QuerySizeLimit.description: The maximum number of items to return.

ResampleDistance.description: The distance in meters at which the input polyline should be sampled to provide uniform distances between points.

ShapeFormat.description: Specifies the precision of an encoded polyline.

fixit_url.description:
A URL where you can submit changes to the source data for a place
(e.g. edit on OpenStreetMap, Foursquare Placemaker, etc.).

source.description:
The source identifier.
The ID of the record as it appears in the original source.

fr_gov_epci_code.description: An Open Data France EPCI code.

fra_insee_code.description: Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques (Insee) code

state_code.description: The abbreviation code for the state (varies by country).

iso_3166-1.description: The ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code of the administrative region.

iso_3166-2.description: The ISO 3166-2 code identifying the principal subdivision (ex: provinces or states) within a country.

state.description: The full state or province name.

attributes.description: A set of granular attributes to include between every pair of coordinates along the route. This can significantly increase the response size.

height.description: The height of the automobile (in meters).

width.description: The width of the automobile (in meters).

toll_booth_cost.description: The estimated cost (in seconds) when a toll booth is encountered.

toll_booth_penalty.description: A penalty (in seconds) applied to the route cost when a toll booth is encountered. This penalty can be used to reduce the likelihood of suggesting a route with toll booths unless absolutely necessary.

ferry_cost.description: The estimated cost (in seconds) when a ferry is encountered.

use_highways.description: A measure of willingness to take highways. Values near 0 attempt to avoid highways, and values near 1 will favour them. Note that as some routes may be impossible without highways, 0 does not guarantee avoidance of them.

use_tolls.description: A measure of willingness to take toll roads. Values near 0 attempt to avoid tolls, and values near 1 will favour them. Note that as some routes may be impossible without tolls, 0 does not guarantee avoidance of them.

top_speed.description: The top speed (in kph) that the vehicle is capable of travelling.

shortest.description: If true changes the cost metric to be quasi-shortest (pure distance-based) costing. This will disable ALL other costing factors.

ignore_closures.description: If true, ignores all known closures. This option cannot be set if `location.search_filter.exclude_closures` is also specified.

include_hov2.description: If true, indicates the desire to include HOV roads with a 2-occupant requirement in the route when advantageous.

include_hov3.description: If true, indicates the desire to include HOV roads with a 3-occupant requirement in the route when advantageous.

include_hot.description: If true, indicates the desire to include toll roads which require the driver to pay a toll if the occupant requirement isn't met

alley_factor.description: A factor that multiplies the cost when alleys are encountered.

maneuver_penalty.description: A penalty (in seconds) applied when transitioning between roads (determined by name).

gate_cost.description: The estimated cost (in seconds) when a gate is encountered.

gate_penalty.description: A penalty (in seconds) applied to the route cost when a gate is encountered. This penalty can be used to reduce the likelihood of suggesting a route with gates unless absolutely necessary.

country_crossing_cost.description: The estimated cost (in seconds) when encountering an international border.

country_crossing_penalty.description: A penalty applied to transitions to international border crossings. This penalty can be used to reduce the likelihood of suggesting a route with border crossings unless absolutely necessary.

ignore_restrictions.description: If set to true, ignores any restrictions (eg: turn and conditional restrictions). Useful for matching GPS traces to the road network regardless of restrictions.

ignore_non_vehicular_restrictions.description: If set to true, ignores most restrictions (eg: turn and conditional restrictions), but still respects restrictions that impact vehicle safety such as weight and size.

ignore_oneways.description: If set to true, ignores directional restrictions on roads. Useful for matching GPS traces to the road network regardless of restrictions.

shape.description: REQUIRED if `encoded_polyline` is not present. Note that `break` type locations are only supported when `shape_match` is set to `map_match`.

encoded_polyline.description: REQUIRED if `shape` is not present. An encoded polyline (https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/utilities/polylinealgorithm). Note that the polyline must be encoded with 6 digits of precision rather than the default 5!

shape_match.description:
Three snapping modes provide some control over how the map matching occurs. `edge_walk` is fast, but requires extremely precise data that matches the route graph almost perfectly. `map_snap` can handle significantly noisier data, but is very expensive. `walk_or_snap`, the default, tries to use edge walking first and falls back to map matching if edge walking fails.
In general, you should not need to change this parameter unless you want to trace a multi-leg route with multiple `break` locations in the `shape`.

bicycle_type.description:
The type of bicycle:
* Road: has narrow tires and is generally lightweight and designed for speed on paved surfaces * Hybrid or City: designed for city riding or casual riding on roads and paths with good surfaces * Cross: similar to a road bike, but has wider tires so it can handle rougher surfaces * Mountain: able to handle most surfaces, but generally heavier and slower on paved surfaces

cycling_speed.description:
The average comfortable travel speed (in kph) along smooth, flat roads. The costing will vary the speed based on the surface, bicycle type, elevation change, etc. This value should be the average sustainable cruising speed the cyclist can maintain over the entire route.
The default speeds are as follows based on bicycle type:
  * Road - 25kph
  * Cross - 20kph
  * Hybrid - 18kph
  * Mountain - 16kph

use_roads.description: A measure of willingness to use roads alongside other vehicles. Values near 0 attempt to avoid roads and stay on cycleways, and values near 1 indicate the cyclist is more comfortable on roads.

avoid_bad_surfaces.description: A measure of how much the cyclist wants to avoid roads with poor surfaces relative to the type of bicycle being ridden. When 0, there is no penalization of roads with poorer surfaces, and only bicycle speed is taken into account. As the value approaches 1, roads with poor surfaces relative to the bicycle type receive a heaver penalty, so they will only be taken if they significantly reduce travel time. When the value is 1, all bad surfaces are completely avoided from the route, including the start and end points.

bss_return_cost.description: The estimated cost (in seconds) to return a bicycle in `bikeshare` mode.

bss_return_penalty.description: A penalty (in seconds) to return a bicycle in `bikeshare` mode.

msg.description: An error message describing what went wrong (if the status is not 200).

time.description: The time in minutes for the contour. Mutually exclusive of distance.

distance.description: The distance in km for the contour. Mutually exclusive of time.

color.description: The color for the output contour, specified as a hex value (without a leading `#`). If no color is specified, one will be assigned automatically.

lat.description: The latitude of a point in the shape.

lon.description: The longitude of a point in the shape.

costingModel.description:
A model which influences the routing based on the type of travel. The costing model affects parameters ranging from which roads are legally accessible to preferences based on comfort or speed.
See https://valhalla.github.io/valhalla/api/turn-by-turn/api-reference/#costing-models for in-depth descriptions of each costing model.

directions_type.description: The level of directional narrative to include. Locations and times will always be returned, but narrative generation verbosity can be controlled with this parameter.

items.description: A subsequent road name or route number after the sign.
The interchange identifier (used more frequently outside the US).
The location where the road ahead goes (typically a city, but occasionally a road name or route number).

elevation_interval.description:
If greater than zero, attempts to include elevation along the route at regular intervals. The "native" internal resolution is 30m, so we recommend you use this when possible. This number is interpreted as either meters or feet depending on the unit parameter.
Elevation for route sections containing a bridge or tunnel is interpolated linearly. This doesn't always match the true elevation of the bridge/tunnel, but it prevents sharp artifacts from the surrounding terrain. This functionality is unique to the routing endpoints and is not available via the elevation API.
NOTE: This has no effect on the OSRM response format.

endNode.description: The node at the end of this edge

intersecting_edges.description: A set of edges intersecting this node.

elapsed_time.description: The elapsed time along the path to arrive at this node.

admin_index.description: The index into the `admins` list in which this node lies.

fork.description: True if this node is a fork.

time_zone.description: The canonical TZDB identifier for the node's time zone.

format.description: The output response format. The default JSON format is extremely compact and ideal for web or data-constrained use cases where you want to fetch additional attributes on demand in small chunks. The OSRM format is much richer and is configurable with significantly more info for turn-by-turn navigation use cases.

banner_instructions.description: Optionally includes helpful banners with timing information for turn-by-turn navigation. This is only available in the OSRM format.

voice_instructions.description: Optionally includes voice instructions with timing information for turn-by-turn navigation. This is only available in the OSRM format.

max_down_slope.description: The maximum downward slope. Uses 1 degree precision for slopes to -16 degrees, and 4 degree precision afterwards (up to a max of -76 degrees).

max_up_slope.description: The maximum upward slope. Uses 1 degree precision for slopes to 16 degrees, and 4 degree precision afterwards (up to a max of 76 degrees).

weighted_grade.description: The weighted estimate of the grade.

length.description: The length of the edge, in meters.

geocodeLang.description: A BCP47 language tag which specifies a preference for localization of results. By default, results are in the default locale of the source data, but specifying a language will attempt to localize the results. Note that while a `langtag` (in RFC 5646 terms) can contain script, region, etc., only the `language` portion, an ISO 639 code, will be considered. So `en-US` and `en-GB` will both be treated as English.

bbox.description: An array of 4 floating point numbers representing the (W, S, E, N) extremes of the features found.

properties.label: 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC, USA
properties.name: 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW

bbox.description: An array of 4 floating point numbers representing the (W, S, E, N) extremes of the features found.

gid.description: A scoped GID for this result. This can be passed to the place endpoint. Note that these are not always stable long-term.

source_id.description: An ID referencing the original data source (specified via source) for the result. These IDs are specific to the source that they originated from. For example, in the case of OSM, these typically look like way/123 or point/123.

label.description: A full, human-readable label. However, you may not necessarily want to use this; be sure to read the docs for name, locality, and region before making a decision. This field is mostly localized. The order of components is generally locally correct (ex: for an address in South Korea, the house number appears after the street name). However, components will use a request language equivalent if one exists (ex: Seoul instead of 서울 if lang=en).

layer.description: The data layer containing the place (e.g. "address" or "venue").

source.description: The ID of the data source that the result came from.

name.description: The name of the place, the street address including house number, or label of similar relevance. If your app is localized to a specific region, you may get better display results by combining name, locality OR region (or neither?), and postal code together in the local format. Experiment with what works best for your use case.

accuracy.description:
The accuracy of the geographic coordinates in the result. This value is a property of the result itself and won't change based on the query.
A point result means that the record can reasonably be represented by a single geographic point. Addresses, venues, or interpolated addresses usually have point accuracy.
Larger areas, such as a city or country, cannot be represented by a single point, so a centroid is given instead.

addendum.description: Optional additional information from the underlying data source (ex: OSM). This includes select fields. The most useful fields are mapped in the definition here, but others may be available.

country_a.description: The ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 code for the country the place is located in.

country_code.description: The ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code for the country the place is located in.

locality.description: The city, village, town, etc. that the place / address is part of. Note that values may not always match postal or local conventions perfectly.

county.description: Administrative divisions between localities and regions. Useful for disambiguating nearby results with similar names.

region.description: Typically the first administrative division within a country. For example, a US state or a Canadian province.

region_a.description: The abbreviation for the region (e.g. PA for the US state of Pennsylvania).

localadmin.description: In many countries, this is the lowest level of government. Sometimes interchangeable with locality.

match_type.description: For search and structured search results, the type of match.

confidence.description: The level of confidence that the result matches what the user searched for. Only available on forward geocoding endpoints.

geocodingLayer.description:
Our database is organized into several layers (in the GIS sense of the term) as follows:

- `venue`: Points of interest, businesses, and things with walls
- `address`: Places with a street address
- `street`: Streets, roads, highways
- `county`: Places that issue passports, nations, nation-states
- `macroregion`: A related group of regions (mostly in Europe)
- `region`: The first administrative division within a country (usually states and provinces)
- `macrocounty`: A related group of counties (mostly in Europe)
- `county`: Official governmental areas; usually bigger than a locality, but almost always smaller than a region
- `locality`: Towns, hamlets, cities, etc.
- `localadmin`: Local administrative boundaries
- `borough`: Local administrative boundaries within cities (not widely used, but present in places such as NYC and Mexico City)
- `neighbourhood`: Social communities and neighborhoods (note the British spelling in the API!)
- `postalcode`: Postal codes used by mail services (note: not used for reverse geocoding)
- `coarse`: An alias for simultaneously using all administrative layers (everything except `venue` and `address`)
- `marinearea`: Places with fishes and boats.
- `ocean`: A really big marine area.

attribution.description: A URL containing attribution information. If you are not using Stadia Maps and our standard attribution already for your basemaps, you must include this attribution link somewhere in your website/app.

query.description: Technical details of the query. This is most useful for debugging during development. See the full example for the list of properties; these should be self-explanatory, so we don't enumerate them in the spec.

warnings.description: An array of non-critical warnings. This is normally for informational/debugging purposes and not a serious problem.

errors.description: An array of more serious errors (for example, omitting a required parameter). Don’t ignore these.

geocodingSource.description: Our database contains info from multiple sources. These identifiers can be used to either limit search results or to determine the relevance of a result.

heightRequest.description: Request body for the elevation endpoint.

id.description: An identifier to disambiguate requests (echoed by the server).

shape.description:
The path to get the height along, expressed as a sequence of coordinates.

REQUIRED if `encoded_polyline` is not present.

oneOf.description:
An [encoded polyline string](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/utilities/polylinealgorithm).

REQUIRED if `shape` is not present.

shape_format.description: Specifies whether the polyline is encoded with 6 digit precision (polyline6) or 5 digit precision (polyline5).

range.description:
Controls whether the returned array is one-dimensional (height only) or two-dimensional (with a range and height).
The range dimension can be used to generate a graph or steepness gradient along a route.

oneOf.description: The decimal precision (number of digits after the point) of the output.

The distance at which the input polyline should be sampled to provide uniform distances between points.
If not set, the input shape will be used as-is.

internal.description: Is the edge internal to an intersection?

link.description: Is the edge a ramp or turn channel?

surface.description: A representation of the smoothness of the highway. This is used for costing and access checks based on the vehicle type.

classification.description: The classification/importance of the road/path. Used for a variety of purposes including fallback speed estimation and access for certain vehicle types.

begin_heading.description: The direction at the beginning of an edge. The units are degrees clockwise from north.

from_edge_name_consistency.description: True if this intersecting edge at the end node has consistent names with the path from the other edge.

to_edge_name_consistency.description: True if this intersecting edge at the end node has consistent names with the path to the other edge.

polygons.description: If true, the generated GeoJSON will use polygons. The default is to use LineStrings. Polygon output makes it easier to render overlapping areas in some visualization tools (such as MapLibre renderers).

denoise.description: A value in the range [0, 1] which will be used to smooth out or remove smaller contours. A value of 1 will only return the largest contour for a given time value. A value of 0.5 drops any contours that are less than half the area of the largest contour in the set of contours for that same time value.

generalize.description: The value in meters to be used as a tolerance for Douglas-Peucker generalization.

show_locations.description: If true, then the output GeoJSON will include the input locations as two MultiPoint features: one for the exact input coordinates, and a second for the route network node location that the point was snapped to.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

sidewalk_left.description: Is there a sidewalk to the left of the edge?

sidewalk_right.description: Is there a sidewalk to the right of the edge?

stop_sign.description: Is there a stop sign at end of the directed edge?

yield_sign.description: Is there a yield sign at end of the directed edge?

not_thru.description: Does the edge lead to a "no-through" region?

forward.description: Is the edge info forward? If false, then reverse is implied.

truck_route.description: Is the edge part of a truck route/network?

bike_network.description: Is the edge part of a bicycle network?

round_about.description: Is the edge part of a roundabout?

traffic_signal.description: Is there a traffic signal at the end of the directed edge?

access_restriction.description: Is there a general restriction or access condition?

destination_only.description: Is the edge destination only? If so, it will not be routed through.

cycle_lane.description: Indication of the type of cycle lane (if any) present along an edge.

seasonal.description: Is access seasonal (ex. no access in winter)?

country_crossing.description: Does the edge cross into a new country?

part_of_complex_restriction.description: Is the edge part of a complex restriction?

has_sign.description: Do exit signs exist for the edge?

bridge.description: Is the edge part of a bridge?

toll.description: Is the edge a toll road?

way_id.description: The OSM way ID associated with this edge (absent in verbose response; see the edge info).

linear_reference.description: A base64-encoded [OpenLR location reference](https://www.openlr-association.com/fileadmin/user_upload/openlr-whitepaper_v1.5.pdf), for a graph edge of the road network matched by the query.

predicted_speeds.description: Predicted speed information based on historical data. If available, this will include 2016 entries. Each entry represents 5 minutes, where the first entry represents midnight on Monday, the second entry represents 00:05 on Monday, etc.

mean_elevation.description: The mean elevation, in meters, relative to sea level.

shape.description: An encoded polyline (https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/utilities/polylinealgorithm). Note that the polyline is always encoded with 6 digits of precision, whereas most implementations default to 5.

names.description: A list of names that the edge goes by.

way_id.description: The OSM way ID associated with this edge.

vehicle_type.description:
The type of vehicle:
* low_speed_vehicle (BETA): a low-speed vehicle which falls under a different regulatory and licensing regime than automobiles (ex: LSV in the US and Canada, Quadricycles in the EU, etc.) * golf_cart: a street legal golf cart that is under a similar regulator regime as the generic LSV laws, but may need to follow special paths when available or abide by restrictions specific to golf carts.

top_speed.description:
The top speed (in kph) that the vehicle is capable of travelling.
This impacts travel time calculations as well as which roads are preferred. A very low speed vehicle will tend to prefer lower speed roads even in the presence of other legal routes.

max_allowed_speed_limit.description: The maximum speed limit for highways on which it is legal for the vehicle to travel. Defaults to 57 (kph; around 35 mph). Acceptable values range from 20 to 80. Highways with *tagged* speed limits higher than this value will not be routed over (some caveats apply; this feature is still BETA).

exit_number_elements.description: A list of exit number elements. This is typically just a single value.

exit_branch_elements.description: A list of exit branch elements. The text is a subsequent road name or route number after the sign.

exit_toward_elements.description: A list of exit name elements. The text is the interchange identifier (used more frequently outside the US).

exit_name_elements.description: A list of exit name elements. The text is the location where the road ahead goes (typically a city, but occasionally a road name or route number).

text.description: The interchange sign text (varies based on the context; see the `maneuverSign` schema).

is_route_number.description: True if the sign is a route number.

consecutive_count.description: The frequency of this sign element within a set a consecutive signs.

begin_time.description: The timestamp at the start of the trace. Combined with `durations`, this provides a way to include timing information for an `encoded_polyline` trace.

durations.description: A list of durations (in seconds) between each successive pair of points in a polyline.

use_timestamps.description: If true, the input timestamps or durations should be used when computing elapsed time for each edge along the matched path rather than the routing algorithm estimates.

linear_references.description: If true, the response will include a `linear_references` value that contains an array of base64-encoded [OpenLR location references](https://www.openlr-association.com/fileadmin/user_upload/openlr-whitepaper_v1.5.pdf), one for each graph edge of the road network matched by the trace.

items.description: A base64-encoded [OpenLR location reference](https://www.openlr-association.com/fileadmin/user_upload/openlr-whitepaper_v1.5.pdf), for a graph edge of the road network matched by the trace.
